coredata.dc:title: Different electrochemical effect on photoluminescence in single-doped/dual-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Yihuai
Huang, Bo
Zhang, Huichao
coredata.dc:description: Due to their application potential in novel optoelectronic devices, several methods have been used to understand or tune the photophysical properties of doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Spectroelectrochemical method is one of excellent means, which has been demonstrated in nitrogen-doped CQDs film to efficiently manipulate their photoluminescence. However, the role of different doping elements are still unclear. Therefore, in this work the optical properties of sulfur-doped CQDs (S-CQDs), sulfur-nitrogen co-doped CQDs (S,N-CQDs) and fluorine-nitrogen co-doped CQDs (F,N-CQDs) films were investigated by spectroelectrochemical method. Compared with S,N-CQDs and F,N-CQDs, S-CQDs have a more obvious photoluminescence (PL) enhancement phenomenon under the same bias voltage, and their PL intensity increases by 100% and 55.8% at +2.0 V and −2.0 V, respectively. We believe that the above PL enhancement is related to the weakening of the inter-molecular π-π interaction after electrochemical charges implantation and single-doped CQDs are more conducive to this process. In addition, the onset potential of PL enhancement is also affected by the doping element.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Spectro-electrochemistry
Single-doped carbon quantum dots
Dual-doped quantum dots
Solid-state fluorescence
π-π interaction

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref

Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China ( LY20A040005 and LQ20F050007 ) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Provincial Universities of Zhejiang ( GK219909299001-001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) S-CQDs, (b) S,N-CQDs, and (c) F,N-CQDs. Insets show the corresponding particle size distribution histograms. AFM images of (d) S-CQDs (e) S,N-CQDs and (f) F,N-CQDs films.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra and (b) PL emission spectra of these CQDs in solution.
The high-resolution XPS spectra of different kinds of CQDs: (a, b) C1s and S2p spectra of S-CQDs; (c–e) C1s, S2p and N1s spectra of S,N-CQDs; (f–h) C1s, F1s and N1s spectra of F,N-CQDs.

Spectrally integrated PL intensity (purple) of S-CQDs as a function of applied (a) positive and (b) negative VEC (red, 200 mV steps each lasting 10s). All the PL spectra (under 3.06 eV excitation, 0.5 s acquisition time per frame) are recorded every 1 s and all intensities are normalized to their initial values at VEC = 0 V. Absorbance (green) of S-CQDs near 405 nm (corresponding to the excitation energy of 3.06 eV) during a stepwise scan of the EC potential to (c) positive and (d) negative values (red, 200 mV steps each lasting 10s). All absorption intensities (1 ms acquisition per datum) are recorded every 1 s and compared with the values at their initial moments (VEC = 0 V). The acquisition time and recording frequency of absorption and PL data in all SEC experiments in the following text are the same as those in Fig. 3 (e-f) Double-peak fitting results of PL spectra at three typical moments: VEC = 0 V, +2.0 V and −2.0 V, respectively.

Spectrally integrated PL intensity (purple) of S,N-CQDs during a stepwise scan of the EC potential (red) to (a) positive and (b) negative values. Spectrally integrated PL intensity (purple) of F,N-CQDs during a stepwise scan of the EC potential (red) to (c) positive and (d) negative values. All intensities are normalized to the values at VEC = 0 V.

Absorption intensities (green) of S,N-CQDs near 405 nm as a function of applied (a) positive and (b) negative VEC (red). Absorption intensities (green) of F,N-CQDs near 405 nm as a function of applied (c) positive and (d) negative VEC (red). All absorption intensities are compared with the values at initial moments (VEC = 0 V).

CV curve of (a) S-CQDs (b) S,N-CQDs and (c) F,N-CQDs films in 0.1 M TBAPF6 supported PC, at a scan rate of 100 mV/s. Insets show the dependence of (ahν) [2] on hν for S-CQDs, S,N-CQDs and F,N-CQDs, from which band gap of 3.59, 3.01 and 3.40 eV are deduced, respectively.
XPS data of surface element contents for three CQDs.

head.ce:title: Different electrochemical effect on photoluminescence in single-doped/dual-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Carbon Neutrality and New Energy, School of Electronics and Information, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou, 310018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Carbon Neutrality and New Energy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Carbon Neutrality and New Energy, School of Electronics and Information, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou, 310018, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to their application potential in novel optoelectronic devices, several methods have been used to understand or tune the photophysical properties of doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Spectroelectrochemical method is one of excellent means, which has been demonstrated in nitrogen-doped CQDs film to efficiently manipulate their photoluminescence. However, the role of different doping elements are still unclear. Therefore, in this work the optical properties of sulfur-doped CQDs (S-CQDs), sulfur-nitrogen co-doped CQDs (S,N-CQDs) and fluorine-nitrogen co-doped CQDs (F,N-CQDs) films were investigated by spectroelectrochemical method. Compared with S,N-CQDs and F,N-CQDs, S-CQDs have a more obvious photoluminescence (PL) enhancement phenomenon under the same bias voltage, and their PL intensity increases by 100% and 55.8% at +2.0 V and −2.0 V, respectively. We believe that the above PL enhancement is related to the weakening of the inter-molecular π-π interaction after electrochemical charges implantation and single-doped CQDs are more conducive to this process. In addition, the onset potential of PL enhancement is also affected by the doping element.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), often termed as carbon dots, are a novel fluorescent nanomaterial with size below 10 nm [1,2]. Since 2004, Xu et al. first reported carbon dots in the experiment of gel electrophoresis processing of single-walled carbon nanotubes, which has attracted extensive attention due to its excellent performance [3]. Compared with traditional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs, e.g., CdSe, PbS), fluorescent CQDs have the characteristics of simple preparation, biocompatibility, low toxicity and environmental friendliness, which can meet the requirements of different applications [4–6]. In addition, the interesting luminescence properties of CQDs powerfully encourage researchers to perform deeper explorations. Many previous studies have shown that nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), sulfur (S), fluorine (F) and their coalescence doping have significant effects on the luminescence properties of CQDs [7–9]. For examples, S-doped CQDs prepared from sodium thihydride as S source had a photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield of 69% [7]. The F,N co-doped CQDs prepared from polyvinyl imine and levofloxacin showed pressure-induced aggregation induced emission enhancement and reversible piezochromic behavior [9]. Nevertheless, the emission process of CQDs is complicated, involving doping elements, surface function and crystal structure. Therefore, it is necessary to further study the fluorescence characteristics of doped CQDs.

In the past, the spectro-electrochemical (SEC) method has been widely used to study the PL properties of traditional QDs. Applying an electrochemical (EC) potential to change the Fermi level can not only passivate/activate the surface defect states, but also inject the carriers into the conduction/valence bands [10–12]. Some researchers have noticed that SEC methods can be used to study the luminescence mechanism of carbons dots (CDs) [13,14]. It was found that oxidation or reduction products were produced during the SEC measurement of carbon nanodots (CNDs) solution, resulting in decreases in PL intensity at both positive and negative potentials [13]. And in the SEC measurement of graphene QDs (GQDs) solution, it was obversed that the injection of charges caused the structural change of GQDs, resulting in the emission enhancing at the negative EC potential and the PL quenching obviously at the positive EC potential [14]. These studies revealed the effect of EC redox processes on the PL properties of CNDs and GQDs, providing an important basis for the trap mediation of their emission, and contributing to a deeper understanding of the emission mechanism of carbon-based nanomaterials. Inevitably, the results obtained in solution conditions do not necessarily apply to film samples, as their PL will be quenched when the samples are in the aggregated state. Recently, we studied the relationship between the optical properties of nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) film and EC potential by using the SEC method [15]. The results showed that applying proper positive and negative potentials can not only improve the emission intensity but also reversibly regulate the PL intensity of solid-state samples. The question is whether all doped CQDs can display a similar PL enhancement during the SEC measurements.

In this work, we use the SEC method to study the relationship between the luminescence properties and EC potential in S-doped CQDs (S-CQDs), S,N co-doped CQDs (S,N-CQDs) and F,N co-doped CQDs (F,N-CQDs) films. The PL intensity of S-CQDs film increases by 100% (or 55.8%) and those of S,N-CQDs film increases by 20.7% (or 0%), when the EC potential is scanned to +2.0 V (or −2.0 V). F,N-CQDs film achieve the maximum emission values at VEC = +1.8 V and VEC = −1.4 V, which are both enhance by 6.3% compared with the initial moment (VEC = 0 V). Those unique PL enhancements are caused by the large number of charges injected during the VEC sweep, which weakens the inter-molecular π-π interaction and inhibits the emission quenching. Further analysis shows that there is a significant difference in the turn-on potential of PL enhancement between single-doped CQDs and dual-doped CQDs. In other words, under the same potential, S-CQDs film is more conducive to charges injection, so the increase of PL intensity in S-CQDs is significantly higher than that in the other two kinds of CQDs.

S-CQDs, S,N-CQDs and F,N-CQDs were purchased from Janus New-Materials CO., Ltd. (Nanjing, China) and these CQDs were prepared according to the reported methods.7, 9 16 Take F,N-CQDs for example, the F,N-CQDs was synthesized by one-step microwave-assisted thermal decomposition method [9]. Briefly, 0.2 g levofloxacin was dissolved in 10 mL 5% HCl solution, stirred until the solution was clear and transparent, and then added 15 mL poly(ethylene imine) aqueous solution under continuous stirring for 10 min, and finally heated the mixed solution with microwave oven for 3 min. After cooling to room temperature, the carbonized brown solid was sonically dissolved in ultrapure water and then filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Finally, the filtrate was then dialyzed using a dialysis membrane with MWCO of 500–1000 for three days and freeze-dried to obtain F,N-CQDs powder.

The materials for the SEC experiment, including Zinc oxide (ZnO) nanoparticles (NPs, ∼97%), propylene carbonate (PC, ∼99.7%), tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphonate (TBAPF6, ∼99%) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich. Anhydrous ethanol and acetone were purchased from Hangzhou Shuanglin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Hangzhou, China).

The SEC measurements were conducted in a quartz cuvette filled with electrolyte (0.1 M TBAPF6 in PC). A three-electrode setup was connected to potentiostat, with indium tin oxide (ITO)/glass coated with ZnO NPs (nanograde, ∼50 nm diameter) and doped CQDs as the working electrode (WE). Silver wire and platinum wire were used as quasi-reference electrode (RE) and counter electrode (CE), respectively. Then, the PL spectra of S-CQDs, S,N-CQDs, F,N-CQDs films were measured by using a continuous wave diode laser (405 nm) as the excitation source and applying positive and negative potentials to the samples through a potentiostat. All potentials reported in this work were measured relative to the quasi-reference silver electrode during stepwise voltammetry scans (200 mV potential steps lasting 10 s each). The integration time of the PL spectrum was 500 ms, and the data were recorded every 1 s. When measuring the absorbance, white light was used as the light source, and only the absorption variation near 405 nm was measured. The integration time was set to 1 ms, and the absorption value was recorded every 1 s. All the SEC measurements were carried out at room temperature.

The working electrode was made as follows: ITO coated glass slides (55 × 13 × 1.1 mm) were ultrasonically cleaned by acetone, ethanol and distilled water in sequence. Then, the ITO coated glass slides were first covered with ZnO NPs to avoid quenching of S-CQDs (or S,N-CQDs, or F,N-CQDs) emission by fast charge/energy transfer to ITO [11]. The ZnO NPs were deposited by dip-coating the glass/ITO substrate into an ethanol suspension of ZnO NPs (2.0 mg/mL, one dip for 10 s) and annealed at 150 °C for 10 min. Finally, 0.15 mL of S-CQDs (or S,N-CQDs, or F,N-CQDs) solution (10 mg/mL) was dropped onto the glass/ITO/ZnO substrate, and the S-CQDs (or S,N-CQDs, or F,N-CQDs) thin film was obtained after the solvent was evaporated.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were carried out with a JEM-1400Flash (JOEL, Japan) microscope at an accelerating voltage of 120 kV, and the samples (dispersed in deionized water) for TEM measurement were prepared by drop-casting sample solutions onto 200 mesh thinner pure carbon coated copper grids. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of S-CQDs, S,N-CQD and F,N-CQDs films were obtained on Dimension Icon (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were performed using an PHI 5000 VersaProbe Ⅲ (ThermoFischer, America) X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with monochromatised Al Kα X-rays (
h ν
= 1486.6 eV). Room temperature PL and absorption spectra were recorded by AVANTES spectrometer. A continuous wave diode laser (405 nm) was used as the excitation source for PL spectra, and a wide spectrum of white light was used as the source for absorption spectra. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves were recorded by using a CS300H electrochemical workstation and the scan rate is 100 mV/s. Other experiment conditions are the same as the SEC measurement.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 1 a–c) illustrate that three kinds of CQDs are quasi-spherical and uniform monodisperse particles with average sizes of 3.40 nm (S-CQDs), 4.00 nm (S,N-CQDs) and 7.12 nm (F,N-CQDs), separately. In Fig. 1d–f, the roughness of films for S-CQDs, S,N-CQDs and F,N-CQDs were measured by AFM, and the values are 6.04, 3.22 and 3.62 nm, respectively. The thickness of S-CQDs, S,N-CQDs and F,N-CQDs films were measured by a vernier caliper, which are 10, 8 and 80 μm, respectively. The UV–vis absorption spectra of three types of CQDs aqueous solution are shown in Fig. 2 a. Compared with the weak absorption of S-CQDs at 265 nm, S,N-CQDs and F,N-CQDs have strong absorption peak at 248 and 284 nm, respectively. And these kind of absorption peaks correspond to the π-π* transition of CC bonds.7, 9, 16Another band peaks of S-CQDs and F,N-CQDs at ∼325 nm is attributed to the n-π* transition of CO [7,16]. In addition, the absorption bands appear in the 350–440 nm range for S,N-CQDs and F,N-CQDs should be attributed to the n-π* transition of the aromatic sp [2] containing CN/CO bonds [7,9,16,17]. The emission bands of S,N-CQDs, F,N-CQDs and S-CQDs (Fig. 2b) in solution show peaks at 458.5, 495.3 and 520.4 nm, respectively [17].

To unveil the surface functional groups and chemical bonds, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of these CQDs were measured. Table 1 provides the surface atomic fraction of CQDs estimated by XPS element analysis. Fig. 3 a and b shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s and S2p from S-CQDs. The C1s spectrum exhibits four peaks at 284.8, 285.2, 286.2 and 288.3 eV, which are attributed to C–C/CC, C–S, CS and CO, respectively [7]. The S2p spectrum shows two peaks at 162.2 and 168.3 eV, which are attributed to S–C and SO, respectively [7]. For S,N-CQDs, in addition to the three peaks of C1s, S2p and O1s at 161, 285 and 530 eV, an N1s peak also appears at 401 eV, indicating that S,N-CQDs is doped with sulfur and nitrogen atoms. The C1s spectral band (Fig. 3c) for S,N-CQDs are similar to S-CQDs (Fig. 3a) [16]. As shown in Fig. 3d, the three fitting peaks in the fine spectrum of S2p belong to S–C (S2p3/2, 163.4 eV), S–C (S2p1/2, 164.5 eV) and SO (169.1 eV), separately [16]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 3e) contains two peaks associated with pyrrolic N (400.2eV) and amino N (401.5eV) [16]. The C1s spectra of F,N-CQDs (Fig. 3f) contain three components, C–C/CC (284.8 eV), CO (286.4 eV) and C–F (286.4 eV), respectively [9]. F1s spectrum (Fig. 3g) has only a fitting peak, which attributes to C–F (684.4 eV) bond [9]. The spectra of N1s (Fig. 3h) can be de-convoluted into pyrrilic N (399.1 eV) and amino N (401.1 eV) peaks [9].

In previous work, we measured the SEC of N-CQDs films and obtained a novel luminescence enhancement phenomenon [15]. However, different doped CQDs may have different optical and electrical properties, which requires us to have a more comprehensive understanding of doped CQDs [18–20]. Then, the PL properties of single doped CQDs (S-CQDs) and dual doped CQDs (S,N-CQDs, F,N-CQDs) films are studied by the same method. We measure the PL spectra of all films under the application of a positive (or negative) VEC scanned (200 mV potential steps, lasting 10 s each, 0.5 s acquisition time per frame) from 0 V to +2.0 V (or −2.0 V) and then back to 0 V. To quantify the effect of EC potential on their PL intensity, we draw the integrated PL intensity diagram and normalized them to the function values at VEC = 0 V.

As shown in Fig. 4 a, the PL properties of S-CQDs film are investigated under the application of positive EC potential. When VEC < +0.8 V, we observe no significant modification of the PL intensity. As the EC potential changing from +0.8 V to +2.0 V, PL intensity increases dramatically, and increases by 100% (VEC = +2.0 V) with respect to its initial value (VEC = 0V). During the EC potential sweeping from +2.0 V to 0 V, the emission intensity drops, but the PL intensity at VEC = 0 V still retains about 23% enhancement compared to its initial value. After waiting for 2 min at VEC = 0 V, the PL intensity is only 1.12 times the initial integrated PL intensity. Fig. 4b shows the effect of negative EC potential on the S-CQDs film system. Between VEC = 0 V and VEC = −0.6 V, we observe no measurable modulation of the PL intensity. Upon increasing the potential to VEC = −2.0 V, the emission increases by ∼55%. The incomplete recovery of PL intensity can also be observed after returning VEC back to 0 V. Based on the above analysis, it can be seen that there appears to be a PL-enhanced turn-on VEC (around +0.8 V and −0.6 V) during both positive and negative potential scans, namely, PL intensity increases significantly when EC potential exceeds +0.8 V or falls below −0.6 V. In addition, the PL intensity curves in Fig. 4a and b also show that the PL intensity responds is asymmetrical to the EC potential during its rising and falling phases, and PL response hysteresis obviously at 0V. Whereas, this hysteresis phenomenon is not caused by EC modification of the sample structure, as the PL intensity gradually recovers to the initial state with an extension of measurable time. The above results indicate that the PL spectra of S-CQDs films have obvious reversibility of EC regulation.

A similar PL enhancement is observed in all solid-state S-CQDs during positive (or negative) EC potential as described above. To further understand the PL enhancement mechanism of S-CQDs films, we measure the absorbance of the sample at 405 nm (corresponding to the excitation wavelength of PL) during the positive EC potential (Fig. 4c) and negative EC potential (Fig. 4d), and compared the absorbance with values at the initial moment (VEC = 0 V). Compared with the PL intensity of S-CQDs films increases by 100% (or 55.8%) at VEC = +2.0 V (or VEC = −2.0 V), the change of absorbance is only −0.08% (or −0.43%) at the same VEC. It can be inferred that subtle changes in absorbance are not the main factor for the PL enhancement.

Generally, the PL mechanism of CQDs is a combination of the intristic states and surface defects [21–24]. The intristic states are affected by quantum confinement, edge effects and the recombination of electron-hole pairs, while the surface defects are associated with oxygen/nitrogen containing (hydroxyl, epoxy/ether, carbonyl and amino groups, etc.) functional groups and heteroatomic dopants (N, S, F and P, etc.). Heteroatomic dopants can be incorporated into the honeycomb structure of carbon atoms, such as interstices, vacancies, etc. The oxygen containing functional groups promote the formation of surface states with an energy level between π-π* transition of CC bonds. CQDs with different doping types may have trap states with different energy levels, which affect their band gap and PL properties [21].

For better understanding the PL mechanism of S-CQDs films, their PL spectra recorded under applying different VEC are fitted by the two-peak Gaussian function. For a brief explanation, we represent the fitted spectra with higher and lower energies as EMA and EMB, respectively. At the initial moment (VEC = 0 V, Fig. 4e), the PL spectrum of the solid-state sample is located at ∼535 nm, and the weak peak with center wavelength around 660 nm comes from the interference of CCD spectrometer. The PL peak positions of EMA and EMB are located at 518 nm and 576 nm, separately. With the increase of VEC, the peak and wavelength of the fitted spectra change obviously. At VEC = +2.0 V (Fig. 4f), the PL peak positions of EMA and EMB are blue-shifted to 496 nm and 555 nm, respectively, resulting in a blue shift of the overall PL spectrum. Resemble the phenomenon observed under positive EC potential, when VEC = −2.0 V (Fig. 4g), the PL peak positions of EMA and EMB are blue-shifted to 511 nm and 567 nm, respectively.

Subsequently, we investigate the SEC of heteroatom co-doped CQDs. Fig. 5 a and b shows the functional relationship between the integrated intensity of S,N-CQDs films and EC potential. As shown in Fig. 5a, the PL intensity curve shows an obvious slow decline from the initial moment (VEC = 0 V) to VEC = +1.4 V, and decreases by ∼5.5% compared with the initial value. When VEC exceeds +1.4 V, the PL intensity increases rapidly and reaches the maximum value at the maximum potential (VEC = +2.0 V), which is 20.7% higher than the initial fluorescence intensity. And then, the PL intensity decreases rapidly, and at the end of the scan (VEC = 0 V), PL intensity decreases by 5.6% from the initial value. In Fig. 5b, it is also observed that the PL intensity of S,N-CQDs film is enhanced under negative EC potential scanning. When VEC is scanned from −1.4 V to the next −1.4 V, PL intensity shows a tiny fluorescence enhancement process and reaches the maximum PL intensity at VEC = −2.0 V. During the whole stepwise potential scanning process, the PL intensity at VEC = −2.0 V only returns to the initial value due to the monotonically decreasing trend of sample fluorescence, namely, the increase of PL intensity is almost zero. At the end of the stepwise scanning, the PL intensity decreases by ∼2.6%. As observed in Fig. 5a and b, the decreases in PL intensity at the beginning of positive and negative stepwise scanning are attributed to the effect of photo-oxidation [25].

Fig. 5c and d displays the integrated PL intensity of F,N-CQDs films as a function of the positive EC potential. In Fig. 5c, there is little change in PL intensity as the stepwise potential is scanned from VEC = 0 V to VEC = +0.8 V. When VEC scans from +0.8 V to the next +1.8 V, the PL intensity reaches its maximum value, which increases by about 6.3% compared to the initial value. Almost complete recovery of PL intensity can be observed after VEC back to 0 V. As shown in Fig. 5d, the functional relationship between the integrated PL intensity and the stepwise scan of negative EC potential, it is obvious that PL intensity does not change before VEC = −1.4 V. Not until VEC exceeds −1.4 V does the PL intensity increase slowly, and this fluorescence enhancement process continues until the next VEC = −1.4 V. With the end of stepwise scanning (VEC = 0 V), the PL intensity recovers to the initial value.

Interesting PL enhancement phenomenons are also observed for two types of dual-doped CQDs films under positive and negative potentials. Obviously, for diverse types of solid-state CQDs, the turn-on VEC of PL enhancement may be different. As can be seen from Fig. 5a and b, the PL-enhanced turn-on VEC of S,N-CQDs is about ±1.4 V, while the PL-enhanced turn-on VEC of F,N-CQDs film is somewhat different. The PL-enhanced turn-on VEC under positive potential (Fig. 5c) and negative potential (Fig. 5d) scanning is around +0.8 V and −1.4 V, respectively. After the EC potential is more than the PL-enhanced turn-on potential, dual-doped CQDs appear different degrees of PL enhancement. The PL intensity of S,N-CQDs film increases by 20.7% at the maximum positive potential (VEC = +2.0 V, Fig. 5a), but the PL enhancement increases almost to zero at VEC = −2.0 V (Fig. 5b). This makes it seem more distinct that the PL of S,N-CQDs film is affected by photo-oxidation at negative EC potential. For F,N-CQDs thin film, the PL intensity increases by nearly 6.3% under the action of positive and negative potentials, and the maximum PL intensity values were obtained at +1.8 V (Fig. 5c) and −1.4 V (Fig. 5d), respectively. In order to better understand the principle of PL enhancement, we measure the absorbance of the samples at 405 nm (corresponding to the excitation light wavelength of PL) during the positive EC potential and negative EC potentials, and compared the absorbance with the values at the initial moments (VEC = 0 V). The absorbance change of S,N-CQDs films at the maximum potentials (VEC = +2.0 V, −2.0 V) are far less than ±1% (Fig. 6 a and b), and the variation of absorbances corresponding to the maximum PL intensity of F,N-CQDs films (VEC = +1.8 V, −1.4 V) are nearly 1.3% (Fig. 6c) and −13.3% (Fig. 6d), respectively. Apparently, the absorbance changes of these two solid film samples are not enough to cause such an enhancement of PL intensity, indicating that they are not the main factor of PL enhancement.

So far, in SEC measurements of II-VI QDs and perovskite QDs, researchers have not observed a special phenomenon of PL intensity enhancement caused by both positive and negative EC potentials [12,26,27]. Typically, the PL intensity changes of traditional QDs have the following two cases. On the one hand, if the emission increases at positive (negative) potentials, it decreases at negative (positive) potentials. This is because the trap states can be passivated and activated by applying the EC potentials, and only one type of trap state (electron traps or hole traps) plays an important role in the luminescence properties of QDs [12,26]. On the other hand, PL intensity may also decrease at both positive and negative potentials, which is mainly caused by the charges injected into the QDs to form charged excitons [11,28,29]. Whereas, researchers also failed to observe a similar phenomenon in SEC experiments of CNDs and GQDs [13,14]. Conversely, it is thought that EC potential may lead to irreversible redox reactions in CNDs at both positive and negative potentials, resulting in PL degradation [13]. In the process of negative EC potential scanning, a reversible trap state change occurred in GQDs, while in the case of positive EC potential, an irreversible oxidation reaction leads to a decrease in PL intensity [14]. Interestingly, in the study of N-CQDs film, we find for the first that QDs films with obvious quenching phenomenon while the fluorescence of QDs films could be restored under positive and negative potentials. The PL intensity increases by 340% (VEC = +1.4 V) and 10% (VEC = −1.4 V), respectively, while the change in absorption has been proved not to be the main factor in the emission variation [15]. In addition, through multi-peak fitting, we also find that N-CQDs have multiple sub-emission centers, and the sub-emission centers appear blue shift when PL is enhanced. So, this emission enhancement we observed may be a photophysical mechanism completely different from the previous experiment, which leads us to further study the SEC of other doped CQDs films. It is widely believed that the emission of CQDs contains multiple components, but only our study shows that the multiple emission components of single-doped CQDs (N-CQDs, S-CQDs) are enhanced at both positive and negative potentials. For dual-doped CQDs (S,N-CQDs, F,N-CQDs), we can confirm that PL enhancement occurs under both positive and negative potentials, but we cannot determine the variation of each emission component.

When a positive and negative potential is applied, a large influx of carries can suppress holes or electron traps, leading to PL brightening, but there is competition between holes and electron traps such that this type of enhancement does not exist simultaneously in positive and negative VEC scans [12,26]. As we discussed above, the variation in absorbance of various doped CQDs (Fig. 4c, d, and 6a-d) show that they are not playing a decisive role in either PL enhancement. The injection of a large number of charges (electrons or holes) in the positive and negative scanning process can enhance the van der Waals force between the graphite layers and weaken the inter-molecular π-π interaction which makes it possible for single-doped and dual-doped CQDs to improve the emission intensity [6,30–32]. In fact, the luminescence of S-CQDs film has many similarities with that of N-CQDs, and their luminescence has multiple emission components, which can be fitted into blue emission (EMA) and green emission (EMB). We attribute this emission to the photo-excited electron relaxation of the n-π* transition to the sp [2] energy levels or defect states, giving rise to EMA and EMB, respectively [33]. The former emission has discrete feature because of the quantum confinement of electrons within the sp [2] carbon domains [33,34]. The latter emission is linked with the oxygen-containing functional groups decorating the intrinsic sp [2] domains at the edges and/or on the basal planes [33,35]. Furthermore, the damping of the π-π interaction may result in the fracturing of the π bond and make the electron structure of the main chain carbon may change from sp [2] hybridization states to sp [3] states, which can be explained the blue-shift of EMA emission peak under EC potential [36]. After VEC is applied, the EMB spectrum also appears blue-shift, which is probably caused by the opening of the radiation compound channel with higher defects state level.

To demonstrate the relationship between band gap and PL enhancement, CV measurements was carried out. The CV curves of S-CQDs, S,N-CQDs and F,N-CQDs films with electrolyte (0.1 M TBAPF6 in PC) are shown in Fig. 7 , in which these CQDs films exhibit a reversible reduction process. By extrapolating the curves of S-CQDs, S,N-CQDs and F,N-CQDs films, the onset reduction potential (
E
r e d
o n s e t
) was determined to be −0.94, −0.90 and −0.84 V νs Ag RE, respectively. Then, by extrapolating the (ahν) [2]-hν (where a: absorbance, h: Plank's constant, and ν: frequency) curves, the band gap (E g) of S-CQDs, S,N-CQDs and F,N-CQDs films (insets in Fig. 7a–c) was estimated to be 3.59, 3.01 and 3.40 eV, respectively. Finally, the highest occupied molecule orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energies were estimated according to the literature [37,38]. They are 2.65 eV and −0.94 eV νs Ag RE for S-CQDs, 2.11 eV and −0.90 eV νs Ag RE for S,N-CQDs, and 2.56 eV and −0.84 eV νs Ag RE for F,N-CQDs, respectively. This means that electrons may have been injected into the LUMO levels when the PL enhancement occurs under negative potential scanning, but not in the positive potential case. In other words, for PL enhancement under positive potentials, charge carriers may be injected into some defect levels. However, the injection of extra charge carriers does not necessarily translate into more radiation recombination, as the simultaneous enhancement trend of the two luminescence components does not support this. Therefore, the weakening of the π-π interaction is still the main reason for the electrochemistry-driven PL enhancement of doping CQDs.

As discussed above, there is a significant difference in electrochemistry-driven PL enhancement effect between single-doped CQDs films and dual-doped CQDs films. We notice that their PL-enhanced turn-on potentials are quite different, also. Their values of N-CQDs and S-CQDs films under the action of positive potential are consistently +0.8 V, and the values are −0.8 V and −0.6 V, respectively, upon the application of negative potential [15]. Compared to single-doped CQDs, dual-doped CQDs have significantly higher turn-on potentials. During the positive potential sweeps, the turn-on potentials of S,N-CQDs and F,N-CQDs films are +1.4 V and +0.8 V, separately, while their values are −1.4 V under the negative potential. In other words, solid-state single-doped CQDs have lower PL-enhanced turn-on potentials, which is conducive to more charges influx, inhibiting hole or electron traps and weakening the π-π interaction. In contrast, a higher PL-enhanced turn-on potential probably implies that there are additional barriers limiting the charges influx, as observed in solid-state dual-doped CQDs. Therefore, it may be used as a new judgment basis to evaluate the EC regulation performance.

In conclusion, following the unique phenomenon observed in N-CQDs film, we also observe a similar phenomenon in S-CQDs, S,N-CQDs and F,N-CQDs films, namely, these emissions are enhanced at both positive and negative potentials. This is a phenomenon that has never been seen in the past SEC measurements of traditional QDs films and CDs solution samples. When the EC potential is +2.0 V (or −2.0 V), the PL intensity of S-CQDs film increases by 100% (or 55.8%). From the initial moment of stepwise scanning potentials, the integrated PL intensity of S,N-CQDs films decreases slightly, which may be due to the effect of photo-oxidation. Different from the 20.7% increase in PL intensity at VEC = +2.0 V, the change of PL intensity at negative EC potential is so small, and the increase at VEC = −2.0 V is almost zero. Compared with the other two kinds of doped CQDs, F,N-CQDs films have slightly delayed in reaching the maximum PL intensity, and their maximum emission are both enhanced by 6.3% at +1.8 V and −1.4 V. Further experiments show that those absorbance variations of several doped CQDs films are not the main reason for the emission enhancement. It has been reported in the literature that the passivation of trap states can only facilitate the enhancement of emission intensity in positive or negative scanning. So we believe that the above phenomenon is mainly due to the reduction of π-π interaction caused by the introduction of a large number of charges into these CQDs. These emissions growth of dual-doped CQDs are much lower than single-doped CQDs. From the perspective of PL-enhanced turn-on potentials, the turn-on potentials of single-doped CQDs are significantly lower than those of dual-doped CQDs, which means that charges are more easily injected into single-doped CQDs film, contributing to emission enhancement. Our experimental results show that the PL recovery of single-doped CQDs films is more prominent, while the PL recovery of dual-doped CQDs films is relatively poor under the same EC potentials. In any case, it provides an effective solution for reducing the inherent PL quenching of solid-state CQDs films. At the same time, it also provides a new idea for the application of these kinds of solid-state films in the photoelectric field.

Yihuai Huang: performed the experimental work, conceived and designed the experiments, and analysed the data, Formal analysis, Data curation, The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript. Bo Huang: conceived and designed the experiments, and analysed the data, Formal analysis, Data curation, The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript. Huichao Zhang: conceived and designed the experiments, and analysed the data, Formal analysis, Data curation, The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.
